day_1.video_title: Arrays Introduction | Java Complete Placement Course | Lecture 10

checkpoints.context_summary: The instructor has completely finished demonstrating how to declare an array and use a 'for' loop to print its elements. Now it's your turn: Declare an integer array named 'prices' with 3 elements, and write a 'for' loop to print them.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must declare the array correctly and write a standard for-loop 'for(int i=0; i<3; i++)' to print 'prices[i]'.

checkpoints.context_summary: The instructor just showed how to take user input to fill an array dynamically. Apply this concept: Use the provided Scanner object and a 'for' loop to take 3 integer inputs and store them in the 'studentScores' array.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must write a for-loop and use 'studentScores[i] = sc.nextInt();' inside the loop to assign the inputs to the array.

checkpoints.context_summary: The Linear Search algorithm explanation is complete! Let's test your logic before the video ends. Loop through the 'employeeIDs' array. If the current element matches the 'targetID', print 'ID Found at index: ' followed by the index.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must implement the comparison logic 'if (employeeIDs[i] == targetID)' inside the provided loop, and print the index 'i'.

day_2.video_title: Introduction to Linked List | Data Structures & Algorithms | Java Placement Course

checkpoints.context_summary: The instructor just explained that a Linked List is built using 'Nodes', which store data and a reference to the next node. Apply this concept: Instead of a String, create a Node class that stores an integer named 'ticketNumber', along with the 'next' pointer.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must define the internal state of a Node by declaring 'int ticketNumber;' and 'Node next;' inside the class.

checkpoints.context_summary: To add a node to the front of the list, we must point the new node to the current head, and then update the head. Complete the 'addFirst' method below to insert the new 'ticketNumber' node correctly.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must manipulate the pointers in the correct order to avoid orphaned nodes: first 'newNode.next = head;', then 'head = newNode;'.

checkpoints.context_summary: Adding to the end of a Linked List requires traversing it until you find the last node (the node whose 'next' is null). Write the correct while-loop condition to traverse the list, and then attach 'newNode' to the end.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must write a traversal loop: 'while(currNode.next != null) { currNode = currNode.next; }' and then attach the node outside the loop: 'currNode.next = newNode;'.

day_3.video_title: Stack Data Structure in One Video | Java Placement Course

checkpoints.context_summary: The instructor explained that a Stack follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) rule. To 'push' a new book onto a LinkedList-based stack, we add it to the top (the head). Complete the push logic to point the new node to the current head, and then update the head.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must implement O(1) insertion at the head: 'newNode.next = head;' followed by 'head = newNode;'.

checkpoints.context_summary: To 'pop' a book off the stack, we must remove the top element. In a LinkedList, this means moving the 'head' pointer to the next node. Implement the logic to sever the top node and return its title.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must remove the top element by updating the head pointer: 'head = head.next;'.

checkpoints.context_summary: This is the final algorithmic test! We want to push a book to the very *bottom* of the stack using recursion. Write the base condition to stop the recursion when the stack has been completely emptied, and place the 'bookTitle' at this new absolute bottom.
checkpoints.expected_concept: The user must write the correct recursive base case: 'if(bookStack.isEmpty()) { bookStack.push(bookTitle); return; }'.
